entry.title: How to Integrate ABAP Derived Roles in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Overview

If you are integrating your Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to SAP Build Work Zone, you may face the challenge of exposing derived and composite roles containing launchpad content.

Creating (PFCG) roles on ABAP system, that derive from roles with launchpad content, is mainly driven by the need to differentiate authorization for the same set of applications. This often leads to many roles deriving from the same base role. In such cases, the derived roles are assigned to users instead of their base roles. The derived roles get the content of the role menu, like Fiori catalogs or spaces from inheritance and focus mainly on authorizations and user assignments.

This blog outlines the approach for exposing derived roles from an ABAP system to SAP Build Work Zone.

Related Blog

In the blog How to Integrate ABAP Composite Roles in SAP Build Work Zone you will learn two options when exposing composite roles. The recommended approach for integrating derived roles is the same as outlined in “Option 2: Indirect Exposure via Single Roles (with IPS)” when exposing composite roles.

Integrating Derived Roles in SAP Build Work Zone

When exposing derived roles to SAP Build Work Zone there is a separation of concerns between the exposure of launchpad content, authorization and role-to-user assignment.

For the exposure, you select only the single roles that contain launchpad content in /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE. Derived roles are not supported in the exposure report. This restriction prevents exposing a high number of roles with identical launchpad content. When the content for exposure is processed, the system identifies roles which derive from the selected roles and includes these references in the exposed CDM content (as relatedRoleAssignments). These references are not exposed to the administrator during exposure but used by the Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) and SAP Build Work Zone infrastructure.

 

Identity Provisioning Service and SAP Build Work Zone handle the role assignment logic, ensuring that users with derived roles on the ABAP side get the correct roles assigned on the BTP side. With that, users get access to the application they are authorized for.

Key points

  • Each exposed single role includes information about roles deriving from it and their relationships, but those derived roles are not directly available in the Content Manager on BTP.
  • The IPS ensures that when an user is assigned to a derived role in ABAP, all required single roles (now exposed) are assigned in Work Zone/BTP. For further information, see  Configure Integration with the Identity Provisioning Service and Exploring Role Assignments
  • This approach is supported since SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition release 2022 and later.

 

Conclusion 

Integrating derived roles with SAP Build Work Zone works best with IPS-driven provisioning, as it preserves existing role structure and automated user to role assignment. Without IPS one must manually ensure that corresponding roles in both BTP and ABAP system are assigned, which is impractical in larger scenarios. The IPS based approach also supports the integration of composite roles, which are often used together with derived roles.  In that case only single roles should be exposed. Information about related derived or composite roles is provided though CDM content and processed by SAP Build Work Zone and IPS.

 

Further Information

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition - Restrictions

entry.title: From Manual Counting to Real-Time Visibility in SAP
entry.content:
Some time ago, we worked with a company that provides pipe manufacturing and related services for the world’s energy industry and other industrial applications.  Although the company operates in many countries and has more than 20,000 employees, the tracking of tubular components for low-carbon energy applications was still a manual task before we started working together. This meant that operators had to count materials directly in the field and then register that information later.

Tracking these materials was particularly challenging given the scale of the operation and the environments in which it took place. Tubular components were distributed across multiple plants and locations, many of them with limited connectivity. This made it difficult to capture and update information in real time, increasing the chances of delays and inconsistencies. Over time, these conditions led to inefficiencies, operational risks, and a negative impact on productivity.

Our original goal was to make the process faster and reduce errors. The most straightforward approach would have been to digitize what already existed by replacing the manual input with a mobile application. Although this would have fixed how data was captured, it would not have addressed another important issue: whether that data could actually be trusted and used in real time.

To address the situation in a general way, and not just part of it, we implemented a solution that included not only a mobile application but that was also based on image recognition. Using the application, operators were able to capture images of the materials, and the system would automatically identify and count the tubular components. This allowed the process to move from manual counting to a more automated and reliable approach.

The mobile application was not limited to image-based counting. It supported daily operations in the field, which allowed operators to execute instructions, manage materials, perform inspections, and update statuses directly from the device. Tasks such as inspections adjustments or managing dispatching processes were all carried out within that same application, reducing the need for manual steps and enabling more consistent work. Not to mention that such an application worked both online and offline and was connected to RFID readers and wireless printers. This made things even faster and reduced errors in material handling.

Deciding how information was going to move from the field into the system was another key aspect. Instead of being manually recorded and later uploaded, the data captured through the application was directly synchronized, making it available as part of the operational flow.

All this information was synchronized with SAP ecosystem. Instead of being delayed or manually transferred, the data captured in the field was updated and made available as part of ongoing processes. This meant that material movements, status changes, and tracking information could be reflected more consistently across the system, without depending on later adjustments or corrections. As a result, teams had access to more reliable and up-to-date information, making it easier to manage operations on a daily basis.

By replacing manual steps with a more automated approach, the process became more consistent and safer for the people involved. This was reflected in a 35% increase in productivity and a 47% drop in work-related incidents, but more importantly, in a process that was easier and more reliable to handle on a daily basis.

entry.title: EDOC_ADAPTOR~SET_OUTPUT_DATA, how to change content for KSeF 2.0 Poland FA(3)
entry.content:
eDocuments or eInvoices are common these days. In many countries, companies needs to send invoice related data to government portals due to legal requirements. Even if the target formats are standardized (at least provided by governments), companies might need to change the XML content before sending out.

This blog explains how to do this for Poland in KSeF FA(3) format

SAP provides a BAdI named EDOC_ADAPTOR, which defines a method SET_OUTPUT_DATA. This method can be implemented to change the content before sending the XML document. This BAdI can be implemented for any country and there are many blogs and other resources available explaining on how to do so.

So why do I need to write the n+1 instruction on this?

What's the issue here?

This week, I was challenged with changing the content of eDocuments related to Poland. I already did this in past for other countries, so I felt comfortable in doing this. Create a BAdI Implementation, filtered by country PL, change content and I'm done.

Method SET_OUTPUT_DATA of BAdI provides a changing parameter CS_OUTPUT_DATA. This is pre-filled with the content generated by the eDoc framework.

If one need to tweak the data, CS_OUTPUT_DATA is the one which needs to be change, right?
Right!

But you might notice, that CS_OUTPUT_DATA seems to be somewhat of empty in case of KSeF. It's a deep structure with nested tables, but everything is initial. If you try visualize content of XML in EDOC_COCKPIT, XML shows up. 
Why can you visualize data in EDOC_COKPIT if CS_OUTPUT_DATA is not filled at all? How can I change data, if system doesn't provide the outcome of framework?

CS_OUTPUT_DATA structure is empty

Solution

There is an OSS note https://me.sap.com/notes/3346323/E#Topic_F, which describes the issue and provides a small hint on what you need to do. Surprisingly, the note was released on 27th March 2026. As of this writing, it has been released TODAY. It feels like I'm the first person coming across this issue

 

The content is available in CS_OUTPUT_DATA-INVOICE-INVOICE_CONTENT but in an encoded manner.

How to change the content

  1. fetch data from CS_OUTPUT_DATA-INVOICE-INVOICE_CONTENT
  2. decode base64 content of 1 into XSTRING
  3. convert XSTRING into ABAP structure. XSTRING here is the XML itself
  4. change your data in ABAP structure
  5. convert ABAP structure back into XML XSTRING representation
  6. encode XString into base64 format
  7. return this as CS_OUTPUT_DATA-INVOICE-INVOICE_CONTENT
  METHOD if_edoc_adaptor~set_output_data.
    DATA io_edocument   TYPE REF TO cl_edocument.

    DATA xml_content_in TYPE xstring.
    DATA output_data    TYPE edo_pl_faktura3.

    FIELD-SYMBOLS <output>                TYPE edo_pl_send_invoice_type.
   
    " read data provided by framework
    ASSIGN cs_output_data TO <output>.
    DATA(edoc_content) = <output>-invoice-invoice_content.

    " decode Base64 encoded FA(3) Invoice to XString
    CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BASE64_DECODE_STR'
      EXPORTING  input  = edoc_content
*                 unescape = 'X'
      IMPORTING  output = xml_content_in
      EXCEPTIONS failed = 1
                 OTHERS = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
*    MESSAGE ID SY-MSGID TYPE SY-MSGTY NUMBER SY-MSGNO
*      WITH SY-MSGV1 SY-MSGV2 SY-MSGV3 SY-MSGV4.
    ENDIF.

    TRY.
        " convert XML XString into ABAP structure
        cl_proxy_xml_transform=>xml_xstring_to_abap( EXPORTING ddic_type = 'EDO_PL_FAKTURA3'
                                                               xml       = xml_content_in
                                                     IMPORTING abap_data = output_data ).
      CATCH cx_proxy_fault.
      CATCH cx_transformation_error.

    ENDTRY.

    " CHANGE DATA HERE
    " add your logic here
    " CHANGE DATA HERE

    " convert new output data to XML XString
    DATA(xml_content_out) = cl_proxy_xml_transform=>abap_to_xml_xstring( abap_data = output_data
                                                                         ddic_type = 'EDO_PL_FAKTURA3' ).

    " encode XML XString to Base64 and use it as new output data
    CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BASE64_ENCODE_STR'
      EXPORTING input  = xml_content_out
      IMPORTING output = <output>-invoice-invoice_content.

  ENDMETHOD.

I found no resources about this issue and only one OSS note. I leave my solution here hoping someone else who comes across this situation will benefit. I'm glad if this saves you some hours of reading and debugging

entry.title: Verify General Journal Entries Flexible Workflow, and Extensibility
entry.content:
Journal Entry Upload Requirements

Do you have a custom solution for uploading journal entries with workflow approvals and email notifications? Are you converting from ECC to S/4HANA and would like to bring your custom code back to clean core? Is Fiori part of UX Journey? If you answered yes to any of those, then this blog post is for you.

Custom Code Solutions

Before the advent of Fiori apps, SAP customers had to create their own custom code to manage the uploading of journal entries via Excel. I have worked with customers that have different solution design when it comes to journal upload. They come in various shapes and sizes like LSMW, custom programs, and even third-party tools. Those were the days when functional consultants had to be creative and work together with technical consultants to address the business requirements around journal entries upload and approvals.

Fiori Apps and Extensibility towards Clean Core

In SAP S/4HANA, we now have a set of standard Fiori Apps that can address the same business requirements and maintain a clean core. This blog post (based on SAP S/4HANA 2023) is a result of different customer projects with varying business requirements. It ranges from a simple (greenfield) to highly complex (system conversion) custom built solutions. 

The information presented intends to equip the functional and technical consultants with Fiori Apps and available extensibility when it comes to journal entries. Here is the list of Fiori Apps that needs to be activated and deployed depending on business requirements.

Fiori App IDFiori App NameDescription Tips and Tricks
F2547AVerify General Journal Entries - For RequesterAs a requester (for example, an accountant), with the Verify General Journal Entries - For Requester app, you can create, display and edit a journal entry, and then submit it for verification. In addition, you could upload the general journal entries via EXCEL or CSV file and then submit multiple journal entries for verification. For the rejected ones, you could resubmit for verification. Afterwards, you can track status and whole change history for workflow.This app can be used as the single point of entry for users to upload a journal entry.
F2548

Upload General Journal Entries

With this app, you can upload multiple general journal entries from a spreadsheet or a CSV file. The template for uploading is available in this app. After you successfully finish the upload, you can post the general journal entries directly. Postings can be made in all relevant ledgers for the accounts for which you are responsible. If not all the journal entries in a file are uploaded successfully, you must correct errors, copy the batch ID of the initial upload to the file, and then repeat upload.

You can navigate to this Fiori App from F2547A via the Upload button.

If you need to enforce that attachments should be mandatory, you can hide the Submit button and users have to go inside the uploaded journal entry to upload attachments. The Submit button will only be clicked after attaching the supporting documentation. An error message could be raised if the attachment is not greater than zero.

F2720

Manage Workflows for Journal Entry Verification - In General Ledger

Before you start to verify general journal entries or currency adjustments, you can define a workflow for your business needs and then activate it with this Manage Workflows for Journal Entry Verification - In General Ledger app. All journal entries and currency adjustments that need to be verified go through the workflow when they meet the preconditions that you define in this app to start a workflow.

Depending on your business requirements, you can determine the approver (e.g. based on HR position) without using the Manage Teams and Responsibilities for General Journal Entry Verification. Please refer to SAP note 3603584 - How to extend Verify General Journal Entries with Custom Rule - SAP for Me on the available extensibility. The step-by-step guide can be found in the attachment of this note.

F3932

Manage Teams and Responsibilities for Journal Entry Verification - In General Ledger

With this feature you can:

  • View the hierarchical structure of teams, sub teams, and super teams for these team categories: FGJEV
Maintain values for custom responsibility definitions in a team

If the requester and approver can be managed using this Fiori app, then it should suffice bearing in mind that it might have some limitations and scalability considerations. The extensibility for custom rule can be considered if necessary.

F6957

Custom Logic

With this app, you can implement BAdIs that have been released by SAP. You can start implementing from the Custom Logic app or from the UI adaptation in an application that can be extended.This is where the extensibility can address the determination of approvers via BAdI.
F4637

Manage Responsibility Rules

With the Manage Responsibility Rules application, you can see a list of standard responsibility rules delivered by SAP application teams. These rules are used to determine agents that are responsible for workflows and situation handling.Familiarise yourself with the concept of CDS views.
F4636

Manage Responsibility Contexts

With the Manage Responsibility Contexts application, you can use the list of standard responsibility contexts delivered by SAP application teams for agent determination.

You can also extend a standard responsibility context by adding your custom agent rule to determine agents that are responsible for your business process tasks, objects, and situation handling.

Use this in conjunction with Manage Responsibility Rules

F1306

Maintain Email Templates

Using this app, you can define email templates to streamline your email correspondence. Templates consist of fixed content (such as subject or disclaimer) and variable parts (such as the form of address).

You can put a link in the email notification so that it can be directed to the My Inbox Fiori app. The URL for your different environments can be maintained as variables.

Consider whether it is really necessary to send email notifications (nobody likes a flooded mailbox) or the in-app Fiori notification will be sufficient.

F1590

Configure Software Packages

With this app, you can configure software packages for transporting extension items.

This is necessary for any development.

F1589

Register Extensions for Transport

With this app you, can register extensions for being transported in your production system.

A transport request is still required but this Fiori Apps is necessary for extensions made via Fiori Apps.

The links provided in this blog was at the time that SAP S/4HANA 2025 is already available. Always review the latest features based on your SAP S/4HANA versions. I hope this blog has helped you together with the other available articles in our community.

entry.title: Multistage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release
entry.content:
Most of the large organizations run SAP and more than 75% of world’s transaction happen in SAP. Many of these organizations running SAP have their plants or operations across several countries. It is very common that the company unit in Germany (DE) takes the order for a product from a German customer, but they do not produce in Germany and hence brings the product from their United states (US) plant and delivers the product to the customer.

SAP supports these scenarios for several years with two process flows

  1. Inter-company sales
  2. Inter-company stock transfer order

However, these classic intercompany sales have some limitations . SAP released single stage Advanced intercompany (AIC) sales and stock transfer between 2 entities with S/4HANA 2022 release eliminating those limitations  of classic intercompany sales. Refer my earlier blog post https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mrinalkroy_activity-7021224794213761024-paiE?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAACqeFcBFLp4MZaIFhaLzuKFWx73dXzaU2U to know more about the limitations of classic intercompany sales and single stage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer.

SAP has released multistage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release which will be game changer for intercompany sales for organizations running on SAP. Most of the SAP customers were looking for multistage  feature of AIC to take care of tax , revenue sharing etc. Physical flow of goods may happen from delivering plant of the delivering company to the customer in the selling company, but one or multiple affiliates other than the delivering company and selling company may be involved in these intercompany transactions.

A high-level process flow for multistage advanced intercompany sales with selling company 1610 in Sweden (SE) , intermediate company 1010 in Germany (DE) and delivering company 1810 in United states (US) is depicted in below image.

Presence of

  • transit plant in shipping tab at the sales order line item is interpreted as advanced intercompany sales order
  • financial chain in shipping tab at the sales order line item is interpreted as multistage advanced intercompany sales order

Transit plant is 1010 DE and financial chain ID 9000000002 (1810(US)_1010(DE)_1610(SE)) in the sales order 10005795 are depicted below.

Financial chain ID 9000000002 with Fiori app Manage financial chain shows

  • Delivering plant as 1810 US
  • Intermediate or transit plant as 1010 DE
  • Selling plant as 1610 SE

Once the sales order (1) 10005795 is created , the following documents  are created automatically by VCM (Value chain monitor) .

(2) Supplying company purchase order 4500003607 at SE with intercompany supplier as DE

(3) Intercompany sales order 60001367 at DE with Sold to party as SE

(4) Intermediate company purchase order 4500003608 at DE with intercompany supplier as US

(5) Intercompany sales order 60001368 at US with Sold to party as DE

Outbound delivery (6) is in pending status .

VCM is the backbone of advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer . It supports orchestration and monitoring for end-to-end process flows, executes process steps automatically, depicts dependency of documents (predecessor or successor) in relevant process steps, tracks process steps, detects defects, helps to analyze errors, reprocesses the steps after fixing the error, and checks completeness of individual documents and overall flow.

Once the outbound delivery is created from the sales order and post goods issue (PGI) is executed the ‘Reload’ of Fiori app Monitor value chains shows the process flow as below. Process step DC-GT (7) is executed with stock transfer to valuated stock-in-transit (VSiT). Movement type is 687 (GMDel to Iss Val SiT).

Internal transfer of control dates under shipping tab of outbound delivery are new fields for AIC sales , those have been populated as below manually for the respective sales organizations .

And subsequently following goods movements happen automatically by VCM .

(8) DC-GI (Delivering company) Goods Issue with movement type 687

(9) IC-GR (Intermediate company Goods Receipt) with movement type 101

(10) IC-GI ( Intermediate company Goods Issue) with movement type 601

(11) SC-GR (Selling company Goods Receipt) with movement type 101

Transfer of ownership from the selling company to the external customer is done by populating the date / time in external transfer of control date in outbound delivery.

The last step of goods movement SC-GI (Selling company Goods Issue ) is then executed with movement type 601.

Subsequently , intercompany invoices , customer invoice and supplier invoices are created and the app Monitor value chains shows the process flow as completed with all the documents .

Like to highlight the following

  • There is only one outbound delivery
  • Physical flow of goods happen directly from delivering plant to the external customer
  • Transit plant is only for goods movements from system perspectives with relevant title of ownership
  • Location substituin is possible with Advanced Available-to-promise
  • Process flow can be executed with Extended warehouse management (EWM) at delivering plant
  • Process flow can be executed with Transportation management (TM) with relevant incoterms and freight cost distribution in relevant companies
  • Internal and external transfer of control dates can be populated automatically with configurations , BAdI with business rules / incoterms , Transportation management TM events etc.
  • AIC supports valuated stock-in-transit with relevant transfer of ownerships
  • AIC supports event based revenue recognition (EBRR) , Trade compliance , Product compliance

A completed process flow for multistage advanced intercompany stock transfer is also depicted below.

This blog is based on my personal tests , observations , insights in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. Will appreciate your feedback / comments.

entry.title: Research Survey: Building an AI Agent for Intelligent Order Combination - Your SAP Expertise Needed!
entry.content:
Order management is one of the most critical yet complex processes in any SAP environment. Whether you're dealing with purchase orders, sales orders or production orders, the challenge of efficiently combining and optimizing these orders while maintaining accuracy and compliance is something every SAP professional faces daily. We're in the early stages of exploring how AI could transform this process and we need your expertise to guide us in the right direction.

What We're Building

The Challenge

Currently, order combination processes often involve:

  • Manual review and decision-making that's time-consuming and error-prone
  • Complex business rules that vary significantly between organizations
  • Integration challenges across different SAP modules and systems
  • Difficulty balancing efficiency gains with accuracy requirements
  • Limited visibility into optimization opportunities
Our Vision

We're exploring the development of an AI agent that could assist with intelligent order combination by:

  • Understanding your specific business rules and constraints
  • Learning from historical order patterns and outcomes
  • Suggesting optimal combinations based on multiple criteria
  • Integrating seamlessly with your existing SAP processes
  • Providing transparent reasoning for its recommendations

The goal isn't to replace human judgment, but to augment it with intelligent insights that help you make better, faster decisions.

Why We Need YOU

Your Experience Matters

We're seeking input from SAP professionals who work with order processes daily:

  • Procurement specialists and buyers
  • Sales order management teams
  • Production planners and schedulers
  • SAP functional consultants
  • Business analysts working with order-to-cash or procure-to-pay processes
  • Anyone involved in order optimization and combination decisions
What We Want to Learn

Your real-world experience is invaluable for understanding:

  • Current pain points and manual workarounds in order combination
  • Business rules and criteria that drive combination decisions
  • Integration requirements with existing SAP systems
  • User interface preferences and workflow needs
  • Success metrics and quality measures that matter most
  • Potential concerns or barriers to AI adoption in this area

About This Survey

Participation Details
  • Time Required: 8-12 minutes
  • Confidentiality: All responses are completely anonymous
  • Who Should Participate: SAP professionals involved in any aspect of order management, combination or optimization
What We're Asking About
  • Your current order combination processes and challenges
  • Decision criteria and business rules you use today
  • Desired features and capabilities for an AI assistant
  • Implementation preferences and success factors

Take the Survey

Survey Link - Share Your Experience Here

Your insights will directly shape how we approach this requirements engineering effort and ensure any future solution addresses real business needs rather than theoretical problems.

Join the Conversation

Have additional thoughts about AI in order management? Share them in the comments below! We'd love to hear about specific use cases, success stories or concerns you might have. Please also share this survey with colleagues who work with order processes - the more diverse perspectives we gather, the better we can understand the full scope of requirements.

Thank You

Thank you for taking the time to share your expertise with us. Requirements engineering is most successful when it's grounded in real user needs and experiences and your participation makes that possible.

For questions about this research or to discuss order management challenges in your environment, feel free to reach out through the comments or SAP Community messaging.

 

 

 

Tags

#artificial-intelligence #order-management #requirements-engineering #business-process-optimization #user-research #sap-integration

entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2603 Update: RAP Project Accelerator, Walkthrough, Adaptation Improvements, and More
entry.content:
With SAP Fiori tools 2603, we deliver a set of focused improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

Project Accelerator for RAP generates full-stack SAP Fiori elements applications from text or images

One of the most important additions in this release is the extension of the Project Accelerator to the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). The Project Accelerator has been available for SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) scenarios since 2024 and supports rapid prototyping with AI, based on images and/or a requirements description. With release 2603, it can now also create ABAP artifacts and services with AI assistance, addressing a long-standing request.

This significantly broadens the reach of the Project Accelerator for enterprise SAP development. The feature is available for cloud-based RAP back ends with SAP BTP or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 or higher. For more information please refer to the SAP Help Portal page Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI. A separate blog post dedicated to this addition is currently in preparation and will be linked here once published.

The Project Accelerator lets you generate applications directly from business requirements documents, either text or images.

 

 

 

 

The Project Accelerator for RAP builds on the capabilities already available for CAP.

 

Direct navigation to source in the manifest gives developers a more transparent editing experience

Since the beginning, SAP Fiori tools has followed a clear principle: support development tasks with convenience, but do not hide the underlying technical details from experienced developers. This is why properties and settings in the tooling generally allow navigation to the corresponding source. For annotations and XML-based settings, this already opens the relevant annotation or fragment file directly. For manifest-related changes, however, SAP Fiori tools previously used a simplified virtual representation of the manifest as an intermediate editing layer.

Over time, this abstraction became more of a liability than a benefit. It introduced additional complexity, made parts of the implementation more error-prone, and did not provide enough added value compared to working directly with the actual manifest.json. With release 2603, source links for manifest properties now open the corresponding location in the real manifest and highlight the relevant section directly. This gives developers a more transparent editing experience and simplifies the path from visual tooling to source code. It also creates room for future optimizations in areas such as undo/redo handling and patch logic, which are planned for a later release.

New walkthrough for getting started makes novice developers more productive more quickly

Users who already work with SAP Fiori tools often appreciate the convenience and breadth of what the tooling offers. At the same time, the number of available capabilities can make it harder for newcomers to find a good starting point. To make onboarding easier, we have added a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code that helps users get started with SAP Fiori tools directly inside the IDE.

The walkthrough provides a practical entry point into the most relevant concepts and flows. It guides users through the initial setup and encourages hands-on exploration of the tooling. If you are new to SAP Fiori tools, or if you want a structured way to revisit the basics, try it out and complete the tasks.

The walkthrough can be started from the Help menu in VS Code, choose Open Walkthrough… and then SAP Fiori tools from the drop-down menu.

The SAP Fiori tools walkthrough explains all the capabilities and has hands-on opportunities to explore and learn.

Import key user changes into an Adaptation Project and keep your edits

With this release, when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can now import existing key user changes. This helps ensure that changes made through key user adaptation are not lost when moving to developer extensibility. The feature is available starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

For more background, read about key user adaptation and the transition to developer extensibility.

Additional new features in SAP Fiori tools 2603 and more planned

Release 2603 includes an update of the tools and generator to support ESLint v9. In parallel, we are internally testing a new validation feature that allows more flexible, domain-specific rules and visual integration of fix options into the Page Map and Page Editor. We plan to release this together with an initial ruleset in one of the upcoming SAP Fiori tools releases and cover it in a separate blog post.

There is also an improved support for value help services referenced by an app’s main OData V4 service. Their metadata can now be retrieved during app generation, which enables SAP Fiori tools to support scenarios such as property configuration for these value helps and mock data creation for preview. This work is part of a broader rework of test generation support that we plan to cover in a future release blog.

The MCP server for SAP Fiori can now generate an SAP Fiori elements application for an existing OData service, including services exposed from a RAP back end. It uses saved system connections from the Connection Manager extension together with a system-relative service URI. This means that the authentication flow can remain outside the AI client and the LLM, following the established SAP system sign-in flow. This is one step toward end-to-end, AI-driven, RAP-based application development in Visual Studio Code, and we will continue to expand this path in upcoming releases.

If you want to stay up to date with changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What’s New section in the SAP Help Portal remains the best place to track newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

The SAP Fiori development portal includes explanations and sample code for all the building blocks available for creating SAP Fiori elements applications.

entry.title: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance UAE: Electronic Invoice in Utilities using Extensibility
entry.content:
You want to enable your system to create electronic documents for invoices posted in Utilities using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance. In this blog, we’ll show you an example on how this can be achieved using extensibility.  

Before you begin ensure that you have implemented the SAP Notes listed in 3705171 - Electronic Document Processing for United Arab Emirates - Peppol: Implementation Overview. You can also refer the SAP Help Portal for more information, see Electronic Document Processing.
Ensure you have also implemented the SAP Notes listed in 2736880 - eDocument EU PEPPOL: Overview note for IS-U.

Steps to Create Electronic Documents

  • Create eDocument Class
    1. Create 'ZCL_EDOCUMENT_AE_ISU' eDocument class by inheriting CL_EDOCUMENT_EU_ISU class.
    2. Add the CONSTRUCTOR method in the ZCL_EDOCUMENT_AE_ISU eDocument class to map the 'Z' eDocument IS-U class name to the field ms_edocument-edocument_class. 
    3. Redefine the method DETERMINE_PROCESS to assign the processes AEINV and AEINVIN.
    4. Use the table TFK003EDOC to configure eDocument type against the accounting document type.
    Example:

    Country/Region 

    Application Area 

    Document Type 

    eDocument Type 

    IN 

    Utility Company 

    FA 

    AE_INV 

     5. Add the class CL_EDOC_PROCESS as Friends of 'ZCL_EDOCUMENT_AE_ISU' eDocument class. 

    Sample Code for CONSTRUCTOR and DETERMINE_PROCESS method:  
    Constructor method signature: 

     


METHOD constructor.
 super->constructor( io_source_data                 = io_source_data
                      iv_edoc_guid                   = iv_edoc_guid
                      iv_land                        = iv_land
                      iv_update_task                 = iv_update_task
                      iv_generic_badi_filter_adaptor = iv_generic_badi_filter_adaptor
                      iv_simulate_for_email_option = iv_simulate_for_email_option ).

  ms_edocument-land                   = 'AE'.
  ms_edocument-edocument_class        = 'ZCL_EDOCUMENT_AE_ISU'.
ENDMETHOD.
METHOD determine_process.
    CASE ms_edocument-edoc_type.
      WHEN 'AE_INVIN'.
        ms_edocument-process = 'AEINVIN'.
        ms_edocument-process_version = '0001'.
      WHEN OTHERS.
        CASE ms_edocument-interface_type.
          WHEN 'AIF_PROXY' OR 'STANDARD'.
            ms_edocument-process = 'AEINV'.
*           reference process is 'EUINV'
            ms_edocument-process_version = '0001'.
          WHEN OTHERS.
            MESSAGE e162(edocument) INTO gv_error_txt.
            raise_edoc_exception( ).
        ENDCASE.
    ENDCASE.
ENDMETHOD.

 

  • Create Factory Class
    1. Create a 'Z' factory class to get the instance of eDocument IS-U class by inheriting CL_EDOC_FACTORY class and redefine its method CREATE_EDOC_INSTANCE. 
    Example: ZCL_EDOC_FACTORY_AE_ISU
    2. Configure this 'Z' eDocument factory class in the view EDOFACTORYV using the transaction SM30.

    Source Type  

    Factory Class  

    ISU_INVOIC  

    ‘Z’ factory class name (your factory class that you just created)  


    Sample Code:
METHOD create_edoc_instances.

    DATA: lv_land_iso      TYPE edoc_land,
          lv_error_txt     TYPE string,
          ls_source_header TYPE edoc_src_header,
          lo_edocument     TYPE REF TO zcl_edocument_ae_isu.

    CLEAR: lv_land_iso,
           lv_error_txt,
           ls_source_header,
           lo_edocument,
           et_edocument_obj.

* Country
    ls_source_header = io_source->get_header( ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'COUNTRY_CODE_SAP_TO_ISO'
      EXPORTING
        sap_code  = ls_source_header-land
      IMPORTING
        iso_code  = lv_land_iso
      EXCEPTIONS
        not_found = 1
        OTHERS    = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE e881(b1) WITH ls_source_header-land INTO lv_error_txt.
    ENDIF.
    IF lv_land_iso <> 'AE'.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    CREATE OBJECT lo_edocument 
      EXPORTING
        io_source_data = io_source
        iv_update_task = iv_update_task.

    APPEND lo_edocument TO et_edocument_obj.

  ENDMETHOD.

  • Mapping Class
    1. We have a generic mapping class CL_EDOC_MAP_UBL_ISU. If you need to enhance the mapping logic, you can use the EDOC_ADAPTOR BAdI's method SET_OUTPUT_DATA. For more details, refer to Enhancements for Electronic Documents.
    Alternatively, you can implement your own custom mapping class. 
    2. Configure your mapping class in the view EDOMAPCLASSDETV using the transaction SM30. 
    Example:

    eDocument Process 

    Version 

    Interface ID 

    eDocument Type 

    Source Type 

    Mapping Class 

    EUINV 

    1 

    UBL_21_INVOICE_REQUEST

    AE_INV 

    ISU_INVOIC 

    <Your Z mapping class or CL_EDOC_MAP_UBL_ISU> 

    EUINV

    1

    UBL_21_INVOICE_RESPONSE

    AE_INV

     

    CL_EDOC_MAP_UBL

     

  • Maintain the Required Customizations
    Apart from maintaining Master Data, Customizing and Integration as explained in the SAP Help Portal, you must also configure the company code for the following IS-U source type.

    1.  Activate company code for ISU_INVOIC source type in the view EDOCOMPANYACTIV for eDocument creation.
    Example:

    Company Code  

    Source Type  

    Application Active  

    <Your company code>  

    ISU_INVOICE  

    X  


By following these steps, you can efficiently create electronic documents for invoices in the utilities sector, ensuring compliance and enhancing operational efficiency. 

Don't forget to test it thoroughly. 

We hope this blog post was useful for you! You can leave a comment here or continue browsing our community for more blog posts on SAP Document and Reporting Compliance topics. 

Looking forward to seeing you here again!

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA migration cockpit - Testsystem & Demo Scripts
entry.content:
If you are looking for an SAP S/4HANA system for non-productive use cases such as showing demos, education yourself, or do some testing you can check the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance. 
With the fully-activated appliance there are also exercises and demo-scripts available for the SAP S/4HANA migration cockpit.

The latest Demo Guides for SAP S/4HANA 2025 can be found here.

Archive: Available test scripts for older versions

ApproachDemo GuideAvailable for Release
Migrate Data Using Staging TablesMigrating Bank Master Data2023 FPS02 & FPS01 / 2023 & 2022 / 2021 (FPS 01 & SP00)
 Migrating Product / Material Master Data2023 FPS03 & FPS02 & FPS01 / 2023 & 2022 / 2021 FPS02 / 2021 (FPS01 & SP00)
 Migrating G/L Accounts2023 FPS03 & FPS02 & FPS01 / 2023 & 2022 / 2021 (all FPS)
 Migration Object Modeler Cost Center Texts2023 FPS02 & FPS01 / 2023 & 2022 
Migrate Data Directly From SAP SystemActivity types / cost centers2022 
 Vendors / Suppliers2022 
 Products / Materials2022 
 Migration Object Modeler Cost Center Texts:2022 

 

How to access the system

1.  General information on the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance 

You can find general information on the fully-activated appliance and how to create a system on the following blog-post: https://blogs.sap.com/2018/12/12/sap-s4hana-fully-activated-appliance-create-your-sap-s4hana-1809-sy...

If you are already experienced with the underlying infrastructure of the SAP Cloud Appliance Library (SAP CAL), here is the direct links to the appliance in SAP CAL external for customers & partners.

2.  Check the Demo Guide Blog to access all the Demo Scripts for the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance 

Best regards,

Kirstin

entry.title: Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI Driven Operations
entry.content:
Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI‑Driven Operations

 

Most of the Utilities organization are entering one of the most disruptive decades the industry has ever seen. Aging assets due to modern technologies, rising customer expectations due to digital world, and the rapid growth of renewables are forcing companies to rethink how they operate and manage their system. Many utilities organizations have already started their S/4HANA programs with the hope that modernization will naturally lead to automation, intelligence, and AI-driven insights.

But here’s the reality most teams discover the hard way:

AI doesn’t scale on a messy foundation. And without a Clean Core, S/4HANA alone won’t get you there.

Across utilities in the U.S., APAC, Europe and the Middle East, the organizations making real progress but having  one thing in common: they aren’t just migrating — they’re rebuilding their architectural backbone from scratch of the system or existing design so AI can deliver business value the way they want it to get modern success.

Why Utilities Are Still Struggling

Many utilities organizations are carrying 15–25 years of technical debt. Their ECC  or R3 and IS‑U systems often include:

  • Custom code buried in user exits, BADI, FQ Event and enhancements both implicit and explicit.
  • Hard‑coded logic only a developer or few team member understand.
  • Manual steps scattered across the meter‑to‑cash cycle.
  • Data spread across GIS, AMI, OMS, CIS and legacy and other third-party tools.

The result is a fragmented landscape where every process behaves differently. And that’s the real blocker. Many utilities think their challenge is adopting AI. But the truth is:

AI cannot operate on inconsistent data or unpredictable processes.

What Clean Core Actually Means         

This important point is most of the people misconception that Clean Core means “no customization.” That’s not realistic for utilities, at least for now — regulatory and market needs are too complex, and greater flexibility is still needed, but future may change for above statement is true for now.

Clean Core is not about eliminating logic. It’s about putting logic in the right place.

A practical Clean Core approach means:

1. Keep SAP S/4HANA Utilities standard

Use it as a stable, upgrade‑safe system of record for metering, billing, finance, and core operations.

2. Move differentiation to SAP BTP

Build workflows, extensions, and apps on BTP — not inside the core.

3. Use events instead of modifications

A meter read posted in SAP S/4HANA Utilities should trigger an event. That event can activate workflows, validations, or AI agents on BTP — without touching billing logic.

Clean Core doesn’t slow innovation. It creates the conditions for innovation to scale safely.

Why Clean Core Is the Missing Link for AI

AI only works well when three things are true:

  • Data is consistent, this must have.
  • Processes are predictable, of course this is something to understandable.
  • System behavior is stable; this is bottleneck as well.

Clean Core enforces all three.

Without it, AI stays stuck in pilots’ phase. With it, AI becomes operational. Utilities that adopt Clean Core unlock outcomes which are:

  • Predictive billing to reduce customer call and dispute resolution.
  • Autonomous collections based on customer behavior. Important for utility industries.
  • DER/EV readiness without breaking billing logic, of course main source of revenue generation.
  • Revenue integrity through real‑time anomaly detection.

This shifts from reactive operations to predictive and autonomous ones and trust me this is future of utilities at least from my prospective with AI Era and digital world we are moving in.

Reference Architecture for an AI‑Ready Utility

This future architecture can be understood in three layers:

1.       Transactional Core (SAP S/4HANA Utilities)
  • System of record for metering, billing, and finance data
  • Standardized and upgrade‑friendly
  • Minimal custom code
2.       Innovation Layer (SAP BTP)

Where differentiation lives. Using:

  • Integration Suite
  • Build Process Automation
  • Event Mesh
  • AI Foundation
  • Joule Agents
  • Customer microservices

This is where utilities build intelligent workflows and automation — without touching the core.

3.       AI‑Driven Operations

The outcome layer:

  • Predictive insights
  • Automated processes
  • AI agents managing meter‑to‑cash
  • Intelligent customer interactions
  • Real‑time decisioning

This isn’t just a tech stack — it’s a new operating model.

A Practical Maturity Path: Utilities don’t need to jump to AI overnight. A realistic path looks like which I feel should be:

  • Level 1: Customized Core (Reactive): Heavy custom code, manual processes, limited visibility.
  • Level 2: Clean Core + BTP (Responsive): Standard core with side‑by‑side innovation.
  • Level 3: Event‑Driven + AI (Autonomous): Real‑time processes with AI‑driven decisioning.

The goal isn’t perfection — it’s steady movement toward a scalable, intelligent architecture.

 

 

How to Get Started

Here is practical first steps:

  1. Assess your landscape, identify custom code that can be retired or moved out of the core.
  2. Adopt standard processes first S/4HANA already solves many historical customizations.
  3. Build side‑by‑side by default innovation belongs on BTP.
  4. Prioritize data quality AI is only as good as the data feeding it.

Conclusion

Clean Core is no longer an architectural preference — it’s a prerequisite for AI‑driven utilities.

The future utility isn’t just delivering power or water or gas. It’s managing data, events, and intelligent processes in real time.

Utilities that recognize this shift early won’t just modernize — they’ll redefine how the industry operates.

entry.title: How the condition value (XKOMV-KWERT) is calculated in subtotal pricing conditions
entry.content:
It is not always clear for some end users how the subtotal condition value is calculated.
In the following blog post, I will explain it in details.

Different from what is observed for pricing conditions, subtotal value is calculated in a different way.

For example, in the below scenario, we observe that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has condition value calculated as '10.559,05', but most of the users would expect its calculation as '58,99 * 179 = 10.559,21', considering the quantity as '179':





According to note 0001007110, mainly the following part, subtotal value is calculated in the following way:

...
This consulting note describes how the condition value (the XKOMV-KWERT field) of a subtotal (lines of the pricing procedure without a condition type) is determined in the pricing result.
...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

The condition value of the subtotal is determined in the same way as the condition basis is determined by 'value-related' conditions.
See Note 0000834174, section 2.
...


Then, from note 0000834174 - section 2:

...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

Only step numbers that are smaller than the current reference step can be chosen as reference steps. This is because the condition values of following step numbers have not yet been determined due to the successive processing of the pricing procedure (see above).

If only the from-reference step is maintained, the system internally fills the to-reference step with the from-reference step. In this case therefore only this one reference step is relevant.
If both the from-reference step and the to-reference step (greater or equal to the from-reference step) are maintained, all step numbers are relevant, that are greater than or equal to the from-reference step and smaller than or equal to the to-reference step.
To determine the condition basis, the system now adds the condition values of all relevant conditions that are not inactive with an 'AKLMX' indicator (see Note 836243).
It should be noted that in this case pricing conditions with the inactivity indicator 'Y' are taken into account during the determination of the condition basis, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
In addition, the system also takes subtotals into account, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
...

Therefore, when checking the related pricing procedure, we see that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has 'from/to' steps referring interval of '10 to 80', which refers to pricing conditions 'PR00' and 'MWST', which are the existent ones in the example above.



Hence, subtotal condition value (KWERT) for 'Net value + Tax' will be calculated as 'PR00 + MWST', resulting in '9.868,27 + 690,78 = 10.599,05'.

Technically speaking, this takes place in the logic below:

Include LV61AA55
Form XKOMV_BEWERTEN 
...
      ELSE.
        IF xkomv-kschl EQ space.
          PERFORM xkomv_kwert_ermitteln.
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
...


Now, to calculate the condition amount (KBETR) for subtotals, system performs a backward calculation, as explained in note 0000791944, i.e.:

...
2. In general, the condition rate of a subtotal is determined by dividing the condition value of the subtotal (see Note 1007110) by the condition basis of the pricing condition (YKMENG) ("backward calculation").
...


Then, for subtotal 'Net value + Tax', condition rate will be calculated as '10.599,05 / 179 = 58,99'.


Technically speaking:

Include LV61AA43
Form XKOMV_KWERT_ERMITTELN
...
* price per unit if sum line
  IF xkomv-kschl = space.
    PERFORM xkomv_kbetr_from_kwert USING xkomv-kwert.
  ENDIF.
...


If a different behavior is required for calculating the subtotal condition rate or value, a custom formula should be developed.

I hope this clarifies this recurrent topic.

entry.title: System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA: Downtime Reduction with Technical Options
entry.content:
Downtime is always a topic in most SAP S/4HANA conversion projects. From technical point of view, what can we do to reduce and optimize the downtime? Let me share my experiences with this topic as part of my work in the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team. 

1.   SAP S/4HANA Overview of Transition scenarios 

The transition to SAP S/4HANA is a strategic business transformation that involves moving from legacy systems like SAP ECC to SAP's modern, in-memory ERP platform. This transition is essential since SAP is planning to end standard support for SAP ECC in December 2027, with extended maintenance available until 2030. 

You may know the SAP S/4HANA overview of transition scenarios below. This blog will focus on SAP S/4HANA conversion scenarios only. 

2.   SAP S/4HANA Standard Conversion General Steps in downtime windows 

In a standard SAP S/4HANA conversion, the business downtime window is the period from when the system is unavailable for end users until it will become available for them again. 

Let’s look at the following general phases: 

  • System Ramp Down and Isolation: The process begins mainly with locking all users and stopping interfaces from/to external systems to ensure that no new data will be entered. Those are rather customer-specific steps, please always have your conversion delivery team align in detail with the customer IT and business teams. 
  • Software Update Manager (SUM) Downtime: SUM finalizes the conversion of SAP S/4HANA and switches the system to the new repository, this is the main part from technical point of view which we can optimize to reduce the total downtime. 
  • Finance (FI) Data Conversion: SUM converts FI table contents from the old SAP ERP data model to the new SAP S/4HANA data model. We treat it as a candidate for optimization, but we will not go into detail about it in this blog. 
  • SAP S/4HANA Technical and Application post-processing steps: Those steps are critical to finalize the system to get it ready again for business operations. These steps ensure that all functional requirements are covered with the new S/4HANA data models and that the system's technical infrastructure is stable. .System Validation and Ramp-up: Technical teams and key functional users verify core configurations and critical processes before releasing the system back to the end users. 

3.    Terminology and Approaches 

3.1   SAP S/4HANA Conversion Tools and Approaches (Conversion only) 

Approaches 

Scenarios and how to reduce the downtime 

Standard SUM/DMO 

Software Update Manager/Database Migration Option - Conversion with DB migration 

Depending on whether the source system is on an SAP HANA database or not, we will choose to use SUM or DMO by picking up different endpoints to launch for the conversion. There are general recommendations to work on to reduce the technical tool downtime periods. 

doDMO (Downtime Optimized Approach) 

Downtime-optimized Database Migration Option - Conversion with DB migration and downtime reduction (DB migration partly during uptime) 

doC (Downtime Optimized Approach) 

Downtime-optimized Conversion - Conversion with significant downtime reduction (DB migration partly and data conversion partly during uptime) 

DMO with System Move 

Conversion with DB migration and data center relocation 

DMOVE2S4  

DMO Move to SAP S/4HANA - Conversion with DB migration and data center relocation 

DMOVE2S4 with doDMO 

Combining DMOVE2S4 and doDMO – DC move, S/4HANA Conversion and reduce downtime by DB migration partly during uptime 

DMOVE2S4 with doC 

Combine DMOVE2S4 and doC  – DC move, S/4HANA Conversion and reduce downtime by DB migration and data conversion partly during uptime 

System Transition Workbench 

A guided tool for moving to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition includeing different scenarios 

NZDM 

near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (ABAP)  - Conversion from SAP Business Suite on HANA to SAP S/4HANA,  an advanced preconfiguration mode and feature in SUM with Record and Replay technique to reduce downtime   

NZDT 

Near-Zero Downtime Technology 

3.2 How to choose the most suitable conversion strategy and conversion path  

Scenario 

Source System 

Available Options 

On-Premise In-Place Conversion 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO, doDMO, doC 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

SUM, nZDM, doDMO, doC 

Migration & Conversion to Hyperscalers / Customer's Other Data Center 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC 

Migration & Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC, System Transition Workbench 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC, System Transition Workbench 

What are the factors and criteria for the decision making with different options?  

From technical point of view, it is not a complicated question, see below decision tree. 

At the same time, you can refer to Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me for the general information 

From overall project perspective, there are some important factors such as: 

  • Total project timeline and cost; 
  • Downtime reduction; 
  • Technical prerequisites and functional prerequisites; 
  • Etc. and customer can define by themselves. Here is a delivery example about the evaluation of the approach and tools options with pros and cons based on customer’s requirements and SAP shared the proposal in a high-level picture. 

If still you have doubts, please reach out to your SAP professional consulting and service teams about additional recommendations for the different approaches based on the system data, sizing report, readiness reports and so onSome customers we worked with choose in-place SAP S/4HANA conversion in their data center and migration of SAP S/4HANA with Copy to target SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. If you choose this option, please consider the total business downtime. And right now, SAP provides this with featured option: RISE with System Transition Workbench with scenario: SUM/DMO run in local data center and use HANA tenant copy to achieve this. 

 3.3   RISE with SAP System Transition Workbench  

It provides a pre-planned “route” tailored to your starting point and destination, saving you the time to plan and reach the route. It gives real-time traffic updates and suggests alternative routes, helping you avoid congestion and delays. 

About "RISE with SAP system transition workbench" | SAP Help Portal  

SAP System Transition Workbench is installed by Enterprise Cloud Services(ECS) on a VM Migration Server. You can get seamless support from your SAP delivery team and SAP development teams as it is released and generally available to all customers. 

There is one great introduction blog for this tool from Kristin. 

Introducing the RISE with SAP System Transition Wo... - SAP CommunityThe tool can be used for migration of customer systems to SAP Enterprise Cloud Services (ECS) in context of RISE with SAP. 

  • SAP HANA Tenant Copy 
  • SAP HANA Backup and Recovery 
  • Homogeneous "Update and Copy" 
  • System Conversion using SUM 2.0 (DMO with System Move) 
  • System Conversion using SUM 2.0 (DMOVE2S4) 

Usage Restriction 

  • The above mentioned scenario is permitted for SAP ECS internal operations as well as for qualified migration partners. 
    Qualified migration partners must have earned at least the “Essential Level” designation for the SAP Cloud ERP Private competency as visible in SAP Partner Finder. 
  • SAP provides required automation tooling and configuration of the virtual machine for the migration.  
    Any non-authorized software export of the automation tooling or its configuration is prohibited. 
    Any usage beyond defined usage constraints is not allowed. 
  • The RISE with SAP System Transition Workbench assumes that the target system and component releases are within SAP maintenance. Technical limitations and/or (general) restrictions might require additional preparation or an upgrade to newer releases of SAP S/4HANA related applications, add-ons, or other technical components. 
  • Currently, the RISE with SAP system transition workbench does not support SAP HANA scale-out target systems 

There is also the published success story of RISE with System Transition workbench helping from Piyush and Praveen to customer accelerated on their cloud journey to RISE.   

Accelerating RISE Migrations: CommScope’s Success Story with Transition Workbench Update and Copy - An Innovative migration approach for Rise 

3.4   Near-Zero Downtime Technology 

NZDT and doC are the two approaches as part of Minimized Downtime Services supporting all scenarios.  

As per SAP Note 693168 - Minimized Downtime Service (MDS) , doC is generally available to customers while NZDT is service-based and SAP Global Delivery Hub would have to be involved. Please always reach out to your SAP consulting team about NZDT scenarios since only a  limited number of customers can be supported. 

4.   Second level considerations and SUM/DMO optimizations 

4.1    Technical Downtime Optimization (TDO) app  

 To ensure that the maintenance using Software Update Manager on the production system goes smoothly, test cycles are performed before updating  production. And typically, the updated system is used to validate the upgrade process and identify any necessary preparation steps and fixes. 

To address these concerns, SAP offer the Technical Downtime Optimization App. This app provides multiple features for analyzing the Software Update Manager runtime and improving the maintenance event. 

 

You can refer to more details about the TDO usage: https://tdo.cloud.sap 

SAP Blog : Simply analyzing and improving maintenance events with Technical Downtime Optimization App 

4.2   General Recommendations to reduce technical downtime 

SAP Note 2351294 - S/4HANA System Conversion / Upgrade: Measures to reduce technical downtime 

4.3   General Recommendations on SAP HANA Database 

  • SAP HANA parameter changes will help data conversion performance but may impact productive performance. Therefore, if you apply these changes before entering the technical downtime, don’t forget to revert those changes after the completion of SUM. 
  • Increase the CPU capacity used for delta merges by changing the indexserver.ini parameter [mergedog] -> load_balancing_func value to “LCC / 2”. Remember the original value to restore it later. 
  • Increase the number of merge tokens and merge threads by setting indexserver.ini parameters [mergedog] -> token_per_table and [indexing] -> parallel_merge_threads to a value of available physical CPU cores divided by 2 (but no more than 256). 
  • Increase the parallelism for partitioned tables by setting indexserver.ini parameter [indexing] -> parallel_merge_part_threads to a value of available physical CPU cores divided by 2 (but no more than 256). If the parameter is not yet available in section [indexing], add it as a new parameter. 
  • Please monitor the memory usage of the HANA database server via visual load history of the HANA cockpit, or the relevant monitoring during the execution of critical phases. 

4.4   General recommendations on SUM/DMO parameters 

Setting up parallel processes properly can dramatically increase the performance of upgrades and updates of SAP systems. As best practice, the following should be considered: 

  • SAP Note 1616401 - Parallelism in the Upgrades, EhPs and Support Packages implementations 
  • Hardware resource utilization during Upgrades and Updates 

              SUM settings 1: ABAP PROCESSES (UPTIME) and ABAP PROCESSES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel background processes and activator processes, also number of batch processes scheduled on the application server. The minimum number is 5. We recommend a value that is high enough to enable parallel processing, but small enough that the production system is not affected. 

              SUM settings 2: SQL PROCESSES (UPTIME) and SQL PROCESES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel SQL processes used by tp to execute database statements. 

              SUM settings 3: R3TRANS PROCESSES (UPTIME) and R3TRANS PROCESSES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel import processes used by R3trans to import data into the database. Note that these processes can generate significant loads on the database. 

              SUM settings 4: R3LOAD PROCESSES 

Number of R3load processes used for the EU_IMPORT phases. 

 

PHASES (SUM)

PARALLELISM MECHANISM

ACT_UPG

BATCH

DDIC_UPG

R3TRANS

PARCONV_UPG

SQL DDL

PARDIST_SHD

BATCH

SHADOW_IMPORT_INC

R3TRANS

SHADOW_IMPORT_UPG1

R3TRANS

SHADOW_IMPORT_UPG2

R3TRANS

TABIM_UPG

R3TRANS

XPRAS_AIMMRG

BATCH

EU_CLONE_MIG_DT/UT_RUN

R3load

 

4.5  The classic optimization methods 

  • Usually optimization is started with analysis of files SAPupConsole.log, LONGPOST LOG, UPGANA.XML and APPLANA.XML. 
  • DMO optimization: Benchmark tool for export/import: see blog Optimizing DMO Performance - SAP Community. 
  • Parallelization framework (SHDB_PFW_CONF) - based on hardware resources of the application server. 
  • SAP Note 2353814 - SAP S/4HANA conversion reports in SD: optimize parallel execution of conversion reports: . 
  • For AnyDB to SAP S/4HANA conversion: 
  • Duration files can be re-used and R3load migration logs are analyzed to check if better splitting can be determined, or a different order of packages. 
  • Source database statistics updates and, in some cases index rebuild as well as table reorganization can reduce the runtime. 

4.6    The steps and phases that contribute most during SUM/DMO downtime: 

  

Phase #  

Key Technical Steps 

Description 

1 

EU_CLONE_MIG_DT_EXP / IMP 

Export and Import: Application tables are migrated from the source database to the target HANA database. 

2 

PARCONV_UPG/ 

PARMVNT_APP_VIEWS 

Parallel Conversion: Converts application tables to the new release's structure and activates their nametabs. 

3 

TABIM_UPG 

Main Table Import: Imports remaining data into SAP-standard tables (classes S, W, E, C, G). 

4 

XPRAS_AIMMRG 

Execution of XPRAs: Runs Reports After Import (XPRAs) to adjust data and settings for the new system version. - Data conversion 

5 

JOB_KONV_MIGRATION/ 

RUN_VBFA_MIGRATION 

SFW - Shadow field Migration for KONV-PRCD_ELEMENTS Conversion 

6 

PARRUN_MKPF*_S4 / 

PARRUN_MKPF_VERIFY 

MM-IM conversion 

7 

PARRUN_FILL_EXTRACT_S4 

MM-IM Conversion 

8 

XPRAS_AIMMRG 

Execution of XPRAs: Runs Reports After Import (XPRAs) to adjust data and settings for the new system version. 

9 

Customer-specific XPRAs 

TABIM_UPG_CUST 

XPRAS_UPG_CUST 

Customer-specific XPRAs 

10 

RUN_SDMI_POST_PROCESSING 

Silent Data Migration: A post-processing step that migrates application data in the background after the system is back online, reducing technical downtime. 

4.7   PFW (SHDB Parallelization Framework) settings based on hardware resources of the App server 

SHDB Parallelization Framework  is and ABAP SAP_BASIS framework to simplify the parallelization topic for ABAP applications via PFW instances covering downtime conversion steps.              

The configuration table SHDB_PFW_CONF  

Report/ t_code:: SHDB_PDW_SUPPORT to configure and adjust the parameters or via scripts directly from sap note 2351294 - S/4HANA System Conversion / Upgrade: Measures to reduce technical downtime 

If still you have doubts, please reach out to your SAP professional consulting and service teams about additional recommendations for the different approaches based on the system data, sizing report, readiness reports and so on. 

5. Summary and Conclusion 

Optimizing downtime for an SAP S/4HANA conversion is not just about choosing the right tool; it is about a multi-layered approach that combines strategy, infrastructure tuning, and precise tool configuration. 

By selecting the most appropriate transition scenario (Standard, doDMO, or doC), leveraging the Technical Downtime Optimization (TDO) app, and fine-tuning parallelism within SUM and the HANA database, you can significantly shrink the business impact of your cutover. 

Key Takeaways: 

  • Analyze Early: Use the TDO app and previous mock run logs to identify bottlenecks. 
  • Tune the Engine: Don't settle for default SUM parameters; adjust ABAP and SQL processes based on your hardware. 
  • Leverage Modern Tools: Consider and compare the tools accelerate the journey. 

Ultimately, every system is unique. We highly recommend performing multiple test conversions to validate these optimizations and ensure a smooth, predictable go-live. 

If you have other questions / feedback, please let me know in the comments. 

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team.

entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint

It can make ABAP-specific mistakes (like forgetting RAP locking fields, or using reserved DDIC words). Always review AI-generated code before activating in production. But as a development accelerator and code reviewer, it's genuinely powerful.

References

  • GitHub: mcp-abap-adt — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: mcp-abap-abap-adt-api — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: sap-skills — 35 Claude Code plugins for SAP
  • Blog: SAP Docs MCP Server — Marian Zeis
  • Blog: Claude Code via MCP — SAP Community
  • Blog: Installing and Extending an ABAP MCP Server — SAP Community

I hope this helps other ABAP developers get started. If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to comment below. Happy coding!

entry.title: The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated OPR
entry.content:
The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated Operations

 

A White Paper by Atul Joshi

 

Executive Summary

Utilities today face a paradox: they must modernize rapidly to support digital customer expectations, DER integration, and grid intelligence — yet they operate in one of the most regulated, risk‑averse environments in the world. Traditional SAP landscapes, especially SAP IS‑U, are burdened with decades of custom code, point‑to‑point integrations, and rigid processes that make transformation slow, expensive, and operationally risky.

This white paper introduces The Utility Clean Core Blueprint, a modernization model that enables utilities to evolve their SAP landscape without destabilizing missions, critical billing, metering, and customer operations. The blueprint leverages SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the innovation and integration layer, allowing utilities to decouple custom logic, orchestrate processes, and adopt cloud capabilities — all while keeping the SAP core stable, compliant, and upgrade‑ready.

 

  1. The Modernization Challenge in Regulated Utilities

 

Utilities operate under unique constraints:

  • Regulatory oversight limits downtime and process changes.
  • High‑volume billing cycles require predictable system performance.
  • Legacy customizations make upgrades risky and expensive.
  • Aging IS‑U systems cannot support modern digital experiences.
  • Integration sprawl slows innovation and increases operational risk.
