Most utilities want to move toward S/4HANA Utilities, but the path is blocked by:

  • 10–20 years of Z‑code
  • tightly coupled interfaces
  • custom workflows embedded in IS‑U
  • inflexible monolithic architecture

This is where Clean Core becomes not just a best practice — but a survival strategy.

2. What Clean Core Really Means for Utilities

 

Clean Core is often misunderstood as “removing custom code.” For utilities, it means something deeper:

Clean Core = A stable SAP transactional engine + an agile innovation layer on SAP BTP

This approach allows:

  • predictable billing and metering operations
  • faster innovation cycles
  • safer upgrades
  • cloud‑ready architecture
  • regulatory compliance

In other words: Keep the core clean. Move the complexity out. Modernize without breaking operations.

3. SAP BTP: The Enabler of Utility Modernization

 

SAP BTP provides the capabilities utilities need to modernize safely:

 

Key BTP Services for Utilities

Capability

BTP Service

Utility Value

Event-driven decoupling

Event Mesh

Real-time meter events, outage events, billing triggers

Integration modernization

Integration Suite

Replace PI/PO, eliminate point-to-point interfaces

Extension development

SAP CAP / Kyma

Move Z‑logic out of IS‑U

Workflow automation

BTP Workflow

Field service approvals, credit workflows

Data intelligence

HANA Cloud

Meter analytics, billing insights

Security & governance

IAS/IPS

Unified identity for customer and employee apps

BTP becomes the innovation shell around the SAP core.

 

 

4. The Utility Clean Core Blueprint

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint consists of five practical steps:

Step 1 — Stabilize the Core

  • Freeze custom development in IS‑U
  • Identify Z‑objects that block upgrades
  • Classify custom code into: retire, refactor, or externalize
  • Clean up unused interfaces

Step 2 — Externalize Custom Logic to BTP

 

Move the following out of IS‑U:

  • complex billing rules
  • validation logic
  • workflow approvals
  • customer-facing processes
  • meter data transformations

Use CAP, Node.js, or Java on BTP.

Step 3 — Modernize Integrations

Replace:

  • PI/PO mappings
  • RFC-based interfaces
  • batch jobs

With:

  • Event Mesh
  • Integration Suite
  • APIs

Step 4 — Introduce Event-Driven Architecture

Trigger events for:

  • meter reads
  • billing completion
  • move-in/move-out
  • credit actions
  • outage notifications

This decouples processes and reduces core load.

Step 5 — Enable Coexistence with S/4HANA

Run IS‑U + S/4HANA + BTP in hybrid mode:

  • IS‑U remains the billing engine
  • S/4 handles finance, procurement, asset management
  • BTP orchestrates innovation

This avoids a risky “big bang” migration.

5. Practical Utility Case Studies (Realistic, Practitioner-Level)

Case Study 1: Reducing Billing Run Failures with BTP Event Mesh

A North American utility struggled with billing run failures caused by custom validations embedded in IS‑U.

Problem: Every enhancement point slowed down billing and caused unpredictable dumps. Solution:

  • Move validation logic to a CAP service on BTP
  • Trigger validation via Event Mesh before billing
  • Return results via API

Outcome:

  • Billing runtime reduced by 27%
  • Zero dumps in 3 months
  • Clean Core achieved without touching core billing logic

Case Study 2: Modernizing Move-In/Move-Out Without Touching IS‑U

 

A regulated utility needed a digital customer onboarding experience.

Problem: IS‑U move-in/out processes were too rigid and heavily customized.

Solution:

  • Build a customer onboarding app on BTP
  • Orchestrate workflow using BTP Workflow
  • Trigger IS‑U move-in via API only at the final step

Outcome:

  • Customer onboarding time reduced from 3 days to 30 minutes
  • No changes required in IS‑U core
  • Regulatory compliance maintained

 

Case Study 3: Eliminating 40+ Point-to-Point Integrations

Problem: European utility had PI/PO interfaces that were expensive to maintain.

Solution:

  • Replace PI/PO with Integration Suite
  • Introduce canonical data models
  • Use Event Mesh for asynchronous processes

Outcome:

  • Integration failures reduced by 60%
  • Upgrade downtime reduced by 40%
  • IT operations cost reduced significantly

 

6. The Coexistence Architecture: IS‑U + S/4 + BTP

Utilities cannot afford a big-bang migration. The coexistence model allows:

IS‑U

→ Billing, metering, device management

S/4HANA

→ Finance, procurement, asset management

SAP BTP

→ Innovation, integration, automation, analytics

This architecture supports:

  • gradual modernization
  • regulatory stability
  • continuous innovation

7. Governance: The Missing Piece in Most Utility Programs

Clean Core is not a technical exercise — it is a governance discipline.

Key governance principles

  • No custom code in the core
  • All new extensions must be BTP-first
  • Event-driven architecture preferred over batch
  • API-led integration
  • Quarterly review of Z‑objects
  • Architecture board approval for all enhancements

This is how utilities stay modern after the transformation.

8. Conclusion: Modernize Without Disruption

Utilities cannot pause operations to modernize. They need a blueprint that:

  • protects regulated processes
  • reduces technical debt
  • accelerates innovation
  • prepares for S/4HANA
  • supports the energy transition

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint provides exactly that.

 

By using SAP BTP as the innovation layer, utilities can modernize safely, incrementally, and intelligently — without destabilizing the mission-critical systems that keep the lights on.

entry.title: S/4 HANA How to Get rid of Basic Auth - SOAMANAGER Local Configuration on Client Certificate
entry.content:
The local configuration in SOAMANAGER is also called automatic configuration. Rather than configured manully, the logical port is configured by the task automatically in the background.

However, in this mode, the authentication doesn't support OAuth2. To get rid of basic auth, an option is to use client certificate.

Configuration Steps

let's take sales order notification outbound as an example.

This is the office help document:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/19d48293097f4a2589433856b034dfa5/ed66f965148548a9bb76c24be640d7fc.html?locale=en-US

1. Generate certificate files and upload it into S4 HANA

Please refer to the configuration steps described here 

2. Activate the AIF

TCODE: /AIF/SETUP,  switch off 'test mode'

3. Unpack AIF Content

TCODE: /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT

4. Ensure that the Web service Point-to-Point is active in Customizing

5. Download WSDL file from SAP Accelerator Hub

https://help.sap.com/docs/link-disclaimer?site=https%3A%2F%2Fapi.sap.com%2Fapi%2FOP_CO_SDSLS_ESR_SALES_ORDER_NOTIF%2Foverview

6. Modify WSDL

Add namespace Prefix definitions

xmlns:wsp="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2004/09/policy"
xmlns:sp="http://docs.oasis-open.org/wss/oasis-wss-wssecurity-policy-1.2"

Add a new policy before the wsdl:type section

    <wsp:Policy wsu:Id="ClientCertificateBindingPolicy">
        <wsp:ExactlyOne>
            <wsp:All>
                <!-- Mandate mutual authentication using X.509 Certificate -->
                <sp:TransportBinding xmlns:sp="http://docs.oasis-open.org/ws-sx/ws-securitypolicy/200702">
                    <wsp:Policy>
                        <sp:TransportToken>
                            <wsp:Policy>
                                <sp:HttpsToken>
                                    <wsp:Policy>
                                        <wsp:ExactlyOne>
                                            <!-- Require Client Certificate -->
                                            <sp:RequireClientCertificate/>
                                        </wsp:ExactlyOne>
                                    </wsp:Policy>
                                </sp:HttpsToken>
                            </wsp:Policy>
                        </sp:TransportToken>
                        <sp:AlgorithmSuite>
                            <wsp:Policy>
                                <wsp:ExactlyOne>
                                    <sp:Basic256Sha256/>
                                </wsp:ExactlyOne>
                            </wsp:Policy>
                        </sp:AlgorithmSuite>
                    </wsp:Policy>
                </sp:TransportBinding>
            </wsp:All>
        </wsp:ExactlyOne>
    </wsp:Policy>

add the following policy reference inside wsdl:binding section

        <wsp:Policy>
            <wsp:PolicyReference URI="#ClientCertificateBindingPolicy"/>
        </wsp:Policy>

Update target endpoint

For example, if this is the service endpoint to be consumed:

https://api.example.com:50001/v1/users

 find out below string in the WSDL and replace it

https://host:port/

This is how the WSDL looks like after the modification

7. Create Profile

choose Technical Administration —> Profile

create a name, say Client_Cert

select SSL, then choose client certification

activate the profile

8. Create Provider System

choose Technical Administration  -> Provider Systems.

choose 'create special' , then 'create Third Party System'

input a system name and select the profile defined just now

input the username password for the SAP S4HANA system

DO NOT activate the system. Just save it. 

Open the system in change mode

go to tab WSDL Assignment. choose to upload the WSDL modified.

After uploading the WSDL,  you can save and activate the provider system.

Check connection, you should get this view

9. Maintain 'Logon Data'

click create button

fill in a name which does make sense for future reading

choose User/Password or X.509, select the PSE maintained with .pfx file

activate it.

10. Local Configuration

go to Service Administration  --> Local Integration Scenario Configuration

click save button

Fill up a name which does make sense

Under Service Definitions, add the inbound service SalesOrderBulkRequest_In and assign the profile you created earlier.

Next step, As the service group, choose  SDSLS_A2A_SO_NOTIF

With this service group, you add Sales Order (A2A) and Sales Order - Send Processing Notification (A2A).

For the provider IBC reference, choose the provider system that you created earlier.

select the logon data maintain just now in the next step

confirm and activate the configuration

11. Process Tasks

choose Service Administration ->  Pending Tasks

click process task List

if everything has configured correctly , you should get a view similar to this:

 

12. Check the Logical Port generated

goto ‘Web Service Configuration', search for ‘SDSLS_A2A_SO_NOTIF’

there should be logical port generated with a numberic id

in the security tab, it should be similar to this view

in the transport setting tab, you should see the replacing URL modified to WSDL filled in the URL field

Since the logical port is via local configuration or automatically configured, none of the field is editable. This is a big contrast to the manual configuration. 

Conclusion

1. By using OpenSSL, you should be able to generate the .pfx file which uploaded into SAP S4

2. By modifing WSDL, you should be able to generate the client certificate authentication configured in the logical port.

Reference

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ws-local-configuration..html

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-members/service-group-configuration-in-soamanager-using-ssl-certificates/ba-p/13572482

entry.title: Spot Tender in SAP S/4 HANA 2025+ with Lean Service based Settlement. (Default Charge Type)
entry.content:
This blog post will help readers, customers, and SAP TM users familiarize themselves with the SAP Transportation Management’s Spot procurement functionality in SAP S/4 HANA 2025 with Lean Service based Transportation Settlement.

SAP Transportation Management’s spot procurement is a subset of the Tendering process having 2 broad categories i.e. Contracted and non-contracted carrier.

In my previous blog post I highlighted step-by-step process for one of the many ways in which we can carry out spot tender in version below S/4 HANA TM 2025. Going forward the process has evolved compared to previous version and also based on the business needs and processes.

Below is the link to PART 1 of Spot Tender which clearing calls out detailed process steps:

https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/spot-tender-in-sap-s-4hana-transportation-management/ba-p/13541617

You can read more about SAP S/4HANA Transportation Management -Tendering in the Application Help for SAP S/4HANA
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/c336c907db3742eeb97e9138dd419970.html?locale=en-US

Note: I have carried out the demonstration with relevant screen shot from an internal system for ease of understanding. There’s no relevance or relation to any external source.

Use Case

Use case is for both the customers in Transportation & Logistics Industry (T&L) as well as shippers from Retail, CPG, manufactures etc. who procure services to move their loads or are in business of providing transportation services and have implemented SAP S/4 HANA 2025+ with Lean service procurement.

Assumption: 

The customer is a CPG company and implemented SAP S/4HANA 2025 SAP Transportation Management along with Lean service procurement.

They have yearly contracts with the carriers to move certain capacities on a given corridor/ route. Alternatively, they may also reach out to carriers who don’t have a contract. Contract in the SAP TM world should be referred as FRA (Freight Agreement).

Challenge:

During certain festive seasons the demand on a particular corridor may outweigh the capacity already procured during the start of the year. In this situation, it becomes really very important to meet the increasing demand and ship the goods to our esteemed customers within the stipulated time frame.

Towards the end of the year the procurement team along with Finance would like to analyze the premium paid as transportation services and how it can be curtailed in the upcoming fiscal year. Alternatively, For many years clients have been requesting to book premium cost in a different G/L and replace the std ADJ charge into a more business defined terminology.

In an ideal world, in the absence of the above the cost segregation is cumbersome and involves additional resources and efforts which aren’t quantified. For the leadership as well, this seems to be a non-transparent process with certainly being a  post facto process to adjust the book of records.

Solution:

SAP S/4 HANA TM 2025+ with Lean Service procurement has come out with an out of the box provision to modify the std ADJ charge type during spot tender process which provides step-by-step visibility and more opportunity to drive the tender process both from process as well as financial stand point. (*some limitations and observations shared in the trail blog post)

Scenario:

Let’s move directly at the stage where a FRO is created and subcontracted. Further, we have received a response for an RFQ from the carrier with evaluation completed. We will begin from the step where the user has decided to manually award the carrier.

We start with an assumption that all Tender relevant configs are in place, as the focus is not on setting up the system but on functionality walk-through.

Pictorial representation of the demonstrated functionality:

Basic Tender flow (Representation purpose)

Post Award system will check whether a valid active contract exists or not. (Representation purpose)

Post Award system will check whether a valid active contract exists or not. (Representation purpose)

Note: Let us now consider two scenarios in the case of Award quotation:

  1. Awarding a carrier with whom we don’t have a Freight Agreement.
  2. Awarding a carrier with whom we have a Freight Agreement.

Scenario 1: Awarding a carrier with whom we don’t have a Freight Agreement.
 
Step 1: User is trying to award a carrier and stumbled upon below messages.

The system has hindered the carrier award process and will not allow to proceed further.

Let us analyze how to can overcome the errors.

Step 2: In order to resolve above error, we can be leverage below BADI and implementation as showcased below.

BADI: /SCMTMS/TCC_TEND_DFLT_CHRG_TYP

Here you can observe that I have created a new charge type ZADITYA to replace ADJ. (Charge type created for representation purpose only)

The glaring difference compared to previous blog/version is that system now restricts and aborts the award process which wasn’t the case earlier.

Step 3: Let us try to award the carrier Post BADI implementation.

The popped messages don’t hinder with the carrier award process and carrier is successfully awarded.

Step 4: When we observe the Charges tab, awarded carrier submitted a price of 4500 USD and system has assigned the entire spot tender amount i.e. 4500 USD earlier viewed under Adjusted/Quoted Amount charge type to ZADITYA.

You can further map the Charge type to different G/L to apportion the cost accordingly. (* Not part of this blog Post)

Now let us look at the Flip side i.e. Awarding Carrier having an FRA (Freight Agreement)

Scenario 2:  Awarding a carrier with whom we have a Freight Agreement.

Note: The BADI implementation is still active.

Step 5:Post awarding the carrier, since system determined an FRA, it displayed below messages with no visible warnings nor errors.

Now let’s Analyze the Charge Tab.

Step 6: In this case, the awarded carrier submitted a price of 4500 USD and has an existing agreement with charges amounting to 2000 USD. System subtracts agreement charges from the submitted charges i.e. giving a clear picture of the individual charges incurred.

The glaring difference to note in contacted scenario is that system didn’t replace the ADJ charge type but continues to work as previous version.

You will have to continue to apportion premium charges as part of ADJ charge type with 2 different ways of handling the process for the customer having both active scenarios.

*Will share more insights but at this stage the above is the observed system behavior.

The demonstration in this blog post doesn’t focus on the configs to setup the system nor does it highlight the further freight settlement process.

Hope this article helps all of us understand the upcoming new functionality of the spot tender process in the SAP S/4HANA Transportation Management 2025 with Lean service procurement. There may be multiple different ways to carry out the tendering process but, in this demonstration, I chose to keep it generic and simple.

Thank you for taking time and effort to read this.

Since the tests are carried out with limited resources and purely based on observations, any Feedback/comments in any form will be highly appreciated.

SAP Cloud Transport Management SAP Transportation Management Travel and Transportation SAP S/4HANA SAP Business Network for Logistics

entry.title: International Trade Compliance Is a Sales Topic. Here's How to Own It.
entry.content:
You're in a customer meeting. The procurement lead asks: "How does SAP handle sanctions screening in our purchase orders?" Or the CFO wants to know: "Can we automate our Intrastat filings?"

If your honest answer is "I'll get back to you on that" — this post is for you.

There's a learning journey for exactly this

SAP Learning has a free, self-paced journey called Positioning International Trade Management. It's built for sales and presales — not consultants, not implementers. Just people who need to walk into a customer conversation and know what they're talking about.

 

 

It covers the topics that actually come up: product classification, Intrastat declarations, sanctions screening, embargo checks, export and import controls. All of it positioned around business value, not configuration steps.

What changes after you complete it

You stop losing momentum in trade-related deals. You handle the first conversation yourself instead of pulling in a specialist. You connect SAP's capabilities directly to what the customer is worried about — compliance risk, manual workload, regulatory exposure.

That's not a small thing. In industries like chemicals, automotive, or industrial manufacturing, trade compliance is often a real buying trigger. Being the person in the room who can speak to it confidently makes a difference.

Who should take it

If you're an account executive, presales engineer, or partner sales professional working anywhere near S/4HANA — this is worth your time. Especially if you've ever avoided the trade compliance topic because it felt like specialist territory.

It isn't. Not after this.

Pass it on

Forward this to your team. Add it to your enablement list. If you manage presales or sales at an SAP partner, this is exactly the kind of thing that separates a sharp team from an average one.

Already done it? Leave a comment — what clicked for you, and how did you use it with a customer?

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q1-26 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
As we continue into 2026, I’m back with the next edition of my quarterly blog series covering the latest innovations and highlights in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. Over the last quarter, we’ve extended existing capabilities, introduced new ones, and invested further in usability, integration, and enablement – all with the goal of helping our customers calculate, analyze, and operationalize sustainability footprints at scale, grounded in ERP data. In this edition, I’m covering:

  • Transport Footprint Enhancements
  • Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)
  • Distance Adjustment Factors
  • Transport Analytics
  • Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints
  • Usability Improvements
  • Other Release Highlights
  • Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets
  • Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases
  • Additional Information

Transport Footprint Enhancements

The last quarter brought several enhancements to the ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation capabilities in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. These updates extend coverage to customer-relevant transport footprints, including integration of customer master data, give you more control over the distance calculations used for transport footprints, and provide you with comprehensive analytical capabilities.

Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

In one of my previous posts, I introduced ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation for upstream and internal transport, covering Greenhouse Gas Protocol Scope 3.4 emissions. This capability has now been extended to the downstream side of the value chain, enabling the calculation of customer‑relevant transport footprints and, with that, Scope 3.9 (Downstream Transport and Distribution) emissions.

The calculation also leverages existing ERP business transaction data and builds on the same transport modeling approach used for upstream transport. The foundation of this enhancement is the introduction of a Customer role for business partners. Business partners with this role, including address data, can be replicated directly from integrated SAP S/4HANA source systems, through APIs, or imported via file in the Import Master Data app. The system derives customer‑relevant transport activities from the following business transactions:

  • Goods Issue for Customer (Outbound deliveries of products to customers)
  • Customer Return (Goods returned by customers to company locations).

When business transactions are replicated from integrated source systems, these transactions now include the Customer ID, ensuring correct assignment of customer‑relevant transport activities during transport footprint calculation. In this context, targeted enhancements were made to the Import Master Data and Import Business Transactions OData APIs on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Based on the configurations in the Manage Transport Settings app, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management automatically generates transport activities, determines routes and legs, and calculates footprints per activity, route, and leg, applying the relevant emission factors by mode of transport. The resulting Scope 3.9 emissions are seamlessly integrated into both Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) and Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) calculations and are available in the standard footprint result and analytical apps – all grounded in ERP data rather than manual estimates.

For setup and process details, see Calculating Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints Using Business Transactions in SAP Help. For importing business partners and business transactions, check Configurations for Importing Master Data and Configurations for Importing Business Transaction Data.

Customer Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport footprint calculations in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management derive distances between locations automatically using the Great Circle Distance (GCD) method. Since real transport routes often deviate from the shortest path due to road networks, infrastructure, and routing constraints, GCD can underestimate the actual distance traveled. To reflect this, you can now configure distance adjustment factors for different modes of transport in the Manage Transport Settings app. When routes and legs are generated, the system applies the configured adjustment factor to the GCD, resulting in a more realistic distance basis for footprint calculations.

Before configuring distance adjustment factors, you should review the methodology documentation of your emission factor sources. Some emission factor datasets already include distance adjustments – for example, GLEC v3.1 emission factors for air transport. Applying an additional adjustment factor in such cases would lead to incorrect results.

Also note that existing transport activities aren’t updated automatically. To apply new distance adjustment factors, routes and legs must be regenerated and the relevant transport footprints recalculated. For details, check SAP Help: Configuring Distance Adjustment Factors and Generating Routes and Legs.

Configure Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport Analytics

To complement the transport footprint calculation capabilities, a new analytics app called Transport is now available in the Analytics section of the SAP Fiori launchpad. The app provides a comprehensive, map‑based view of corporate and product transport footprints for a selected footprint inventory scope and period. Transport legs are visualized as connections between source and target locations, with color‑coding that highlights higher‑emission legs. You can filter by product, mode of transport, business transaction type, or location, and switch to a table view for detailed, leg‑level analysis sorted by emission value. The app also supports comparisons across different periods. To use the app, you need the SUS_PFM_Calculate_Transport_Footprints_Read role. See SAP Help for more information: Analyzing Footprints for Transport.

Transport Analytics

To reflect all those updates, the process steps for calculating transport footprints using business transaction data have also been updated in the Scenario Guide: Process 8 – Calculating Scope 3.4 and Scope 3.9 Emissions Using Business Transaction Data.

 

Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints

Until now, all purchased product footprints covered in the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app were automatically attributed to GHG Protocol Scope 3.1 (Purchased Goods and Services) in corporate carbon footprint calculations. For most companies, this is the correct assignment – but not in all cases. Capital goods such as machinery, equipment, or buildings that are purchased and used over multiple years belong to Scope 3.2 (Capital Goods). Correctly distinguishing between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2 is important both for GHG Protocol compliance and for maintaining a high‑quality footprint inventory. This is also a feature that was requested by customers via SAP Influence.

With a recent enhancement, you can now explicitly assign a purchased product footprint or product group footprint to either:

  • Scope 3.1 – Purchased Goods and Services, or
  • Scope 3.2 – Capital Goods

This categorization is supported consistently across all relevant workflows in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management:

  • When creating purchased product footprints manually,
  • When mapping emission factors manually or using AI‑based mapping, and
  • When importing purchased product footprints via data files.

Scope 3.2 Categorization

This enhancement ensures that each purchased product footprint is correctly attributed when calculating your organizational footprint inventory, resulting in a more accurate and auditable split between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2. Existing purchased product footprints that were previously categorized automatically remain assigned to Scope 3.1 by default, ensuring backward compatibility. For more information, see Creating Footprints for Purchased Products and Product Groups in SAP Help.

For analyzing your calculated footprints, the updated categorization is also reflected correctly in the Footprint Overview app, where purchased product footprints assigned to Scope 3.2 are shown separately from Scope 3.1.

 

Usability Improvements

To support more efficient day‑to‑day work with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we continuously invest in improving usability and increasing automation. Last quarter, we introduced several enhancements around form‑based data collection, manual data imports, and better transparency in integrations. These improvements focus on reducing manual effort in recurring data collection tasks, simplifying bulk operations, and making changes and corrections easier to manage at scale – especially in scenarios with large numbers of forms, users, or integrated systems. Many of these enhancements are the direct result of customer feedback, collected through SAP Influence as well as ongoing conversations with customers and partners.

You can now change the status of GHG data forms directly during data import when using spreadsheet templates in the Record GHG Data and Manage GHG Data Collections apps. GHG forms can be set to Applicable or Not Applicable as part of the import. When setting a form to Not Applicable, a comment is required, consistent with the behavior in the user interface. This applies to both bulk templates on the main page of the apps and the individual templates per GHG form type, streamlining bulk updates and reducing manual follow‑up steps.

To further automate and improve correction workflows, GHG data forms that were previously set to Completed or Not Applicable can now be reopened via API using the POST/massUpdate action in the GHG Data Forms API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This provides additional flexibility for automated correction scenarios and integration workflows where reported data needs to be revised after submission.

For providing a more consistent and efficient user experience, downloaded templates now better reflect the UI and applied filters. Template sorting now mirrors the UI table sorting, and templates can be downloaded together with the currently applied UI filters. These enhancements apply to apps such as Manage Planned Energy Consumption Rates, Manage Business Activities, Model Energy Flows, Manage Waste Footprints, Record GHG Data, Manage Transport Operation Footprints, Manage Allocations, Manage Purchased Product Footprints, and Manage Transport Settings. In the Calculate Footprints, Model Energy Flows, Record GHG Data, and Manage GHG Data Collections apps, downloading templates together with the displayed data is now the default behavior.

Finally, the sustainability data integration apps now include a Change History tab in Manage Replications, Manage Source Systems, and Configure Extensions for Replications. This allows you to track who made which changes and when, including the old and new values for edited fields, improving transparency and traceability when managing replication configurations.

 

Other Release Highlights

When performing a current period calculation, input items for footprint items of type Product that only contribute a defined CO₂e value without a product quantity are no longer considered in the per‑unit calculation. These input items are excluded because they have no associated product quantity and would otherwise distort per‑unit results, for example transports or allocations. Their CO₂e values are still fully aggregated into the total footprint. When using CO₂e‑only input items, make sure they’re scaled to the total product quantity where appropriate.

The system now supports goods receipt from production even without production history such as goods issues to production, work‑in‑progress postings, or internal service confirmations in the current period. In these cases, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management uses the previous period footprint per unit of the produced product or, if not available, maintained purchased product footprints. This eliminates previous calculation errors and applies the product footprint determination methods defined in the calculation variant.

Custom temperature conditions can now be replicated from source systems using the Manage Replications app. This applies to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition source systems. For pull‑based replication scenarios, the setup requires custom CDS views in the source system.

Lastly, we’re also continuously enhancing our documentation on SAP Help to improve transparency and onboarding. The Implement – Getting Started section now includes a link to ERP Objects, providing details on relevant CDS views, tables, and fields in SAP S/4HANA for each supported master data entity. In addition, the Product Carbon Footprint Scenario Guide has been extended with step‑by‑step processes for calculating product carbon footprints for cradle‑to‑gate and cradle‑to‑grave scenarios, including concrete examples for allocating Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using business activities, allocations, and business transaction data.

 

Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets

One of the persistent challenges in Scope 3 reporting is the gap between supplier‑specific data and the global average emission factors that many companies fall back on when that data isn’t available. Those averages can be misleading. For agricultural commodities, for instance, emission factors can vary by up to 200% depending on the country of origin, reflecting differences in land‑use change, energy consumption, and local production practices. The wider the gap between your actual supply chain and the averages you apply, the less reliable your footprint calculations become. To overcome this challenge, we onboarded a new addition to the SAP partner ecosystem: Unibloom. Unibloom’s country‑specific supply‑chain emissions datasets are now available on SAP Store and can be integrated directly with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

Unibloom provides ISO 14044‑aligned, country‑specific emission factors for agricultural and industrial commodities, including land‑use‑ and management‑adjusted data. The coverage spans key sectors such as SBTi FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture). The datasets are delivered in a ready‑to‑import format and can be extended on demand with additional country‑specific factors for specific materials, ingredients, or industries. With Unibloom datasets in place, you can replace broad global averages with granular, location‑specific emission factors in your footprint calculations. This improves accuracy and auditability, increases confidence in reported results, and supports more informed sourcing and procurement decisions.

To go deeper into the datasets and their practical use with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we’re hosting a joint webinar with Unibloom. The session focuses on improving Scope 3 accuracy for agricultural and FLAG‑relevant value chains and shows how country‑specific emission factors can be applied in practice. Access the webinar and register here: End data paralysis. Know when your Scope 3 data is good enough.

 

Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases

To support our customers in moving beyond pure carbon metrics, we introduced multi‑impact environmental footprints in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management last quarter – I covered the core capability in more detail in my deep dive.

To help you apply this capability in practice, consistently across different reporting frameworks and industry requirements, we’ve invested in further enablement resources. As a starting point, a new Learning Journey provides a structured introduction to the topic: Establishing the Multi-impact Environmental Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. It walks through the underlying concepts, configuration, and usage of multi‑impact footprints, providing the context for how additional environmental impact categories can be calculated, managed, and analyzed alongside carbon, using a single, consistent data model.

With the basics in place, the focus is now shifting from how multi‑impact categories work to how they can be applied in practice for concrete reporting frameworks and industry requirements. In this context, my colleague @Cielo_SanabriaRuiz has published two follow‑up posts that demonstrate exactly that:

The first, SBTi Forest, Land, and Agriculture (FLAG) in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, explains why distinguishing land‑based emissions from fossil emissions matters at both corporate and product level. It walks through the specific impact categories and emission factors required for SBTi FLAG compliance and includes a mapping table that links impact categories to FLAG reporting categories.

The second, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for the Chemicals (Together for Sustainability), focuses on the chemical industry and the Together for Sustainability (TfS) guideline. It covers the sector‑specific setup for product carbon footprints and Scope 3.1 calculations, including allocation rules for multi‑output processes, transport requirements such as the Radiative Forcing Index (RFI) for air transport, and a complete mapping of impact categories to TfS reporting standards.

Taken together, this enablement material shows how multi‑impact footprints can be used to support FLAG and TfS reporting requirements without fragmenting data models or calculation logic, while still staying aligned with established standards. If you’re working on FLAG reporting, chemical‑industry footprinting, or more broadly on extending footprint calculations beyond carbon, these resources provide a coherent path from concepts to practical application.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release. To continue receiving e-mail notifications, you need to subscribe again – just visit the page and choose Subscribe.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through all the features and functions comprehensively. Recently, we also added new tours and will shortly release additional ones, representing the latest features and an enhanced user experience.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: Reimagining the Customer Journey with AI-Powered SAP Sales and Service Excellence
entry.content:
📌  Introduction

How modern enterprises are weaving together intelligent automation, real-time knowledge, and seamless cross-platform workflows to deliver frictionless customer experiences.

In today’s digital-first world, customers expect fast, personalized, and seamless service experiences. Traditional disconnected systems often lead to delays, inefficiencies, and poor customer satisfaction.

To address this, organizations are adopting intelligent, integrated solutions that connect sales, service, and backend operations. By leveraging platforms like SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and SAP Field Service Management, along with AI capabilities, businesses can deliver a unified and proactive customer journey.

This blog demonstrates an end-to-end process that integrates AI, automation, and enterprise applications to optimize customer experience from initiation to closure.

💼  Business Use Case

Consider a scenario where a customer encounters an issue with a product and begins by attempting to resolve it through a self-service portal. If the issue remains unresolved, a case is created and handled by a service agent, supported by intelligent recommendations and knowledge systems. As the interaction progresses, the situation may evolve into a potential sales opportunity, enabling the organization to propose a suitable solution or upgrade. A quote is then generated and shared with the customer, who can review and sign it digitally, ensuring a seamless and efficient agreement process.

Once confirmed, backend systems take over to process the order and initiate service fulfillment. This includes creating the necessary orders and coordinating with field service operations, where a technician is assigned for on-site support. The technician completes the required service, resolves the issue, and updates the system, leading to the closure of the case.

This end-to-end use case demonstrates how organizations can transform traditional support interactions into revenue-generating opportunities, while automating backend processes, improving response and resolution times, and delivering a truly seamless, cross-functional customer experience.

🏗️   Architecture

The solution integrates multiple systems across the customer journey:


Frontend & Experience Layer
SAP Build Apps
• Self-service capabilities for customers
Customer Engagement Layer
SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2
• Case Management • Opportunity Management • Quote Management • AI-assisted troubleshooting
AI & Knowledge Layer
eGain Knowledge Management
• Intelligent Q&A • Knowledge Base search • Similar case suggestions
Digital Signature Integration
DocuSign
• Seamless agreement processing • Digital contract signing
Backend ERP Layer
SAP S/4HANA Cloud
• Solution Order • Sales Order • Delivery • Billing
Field Service Layer
SAP Field Service Management
• Service Call • Technician Assignment • Field execution

⚙️  Key Capabilities and Business Benefits

  • 🤖AI-Driven Self-Service : Automated issue identification and resolution suggestions
  • 📚Intelligent Knowledge Management : Context-aware recommendations using knowledge base
  • 🔄End-to-End Process Integration : Seamless flow from case → opportunity → quote → order → service
  • ✍️Digital Contract Management : Faster approvals using DocuSign
  • 🔧Smart Field Service Execution : Automated technician assignment and scheduling

📋  Prerequisites

Before implementing this end-to-end solution, it’s important to have the right landscape, integrations, and development setup in place.

🔧  Core Systems

Ensure access to the following key solutions:

  • SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 for case, opportunity, and quote management
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud for backend order processing and billing
  • SAP Field Service Management for service execution and technician handling

🔌  Integration Layer

  • Middleware or API-based integration using SAP BTP (recommended)
  • Connectivity with DocuSign for digital agreements
  • Integration with eGain Knowledge Management for knowledge and AI-driven recommendations

🧠 AI Enablement

  • Configured and structured knowledge base
  • AI services enabled for intelligent Q&A, recommendations, and case insights

👨‍💻Development & Extension Setup

  • Use CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) to build custom microservices, autoflow logic, and backend extensions on SAP BTP
  • Develop UI applications using SAP Fiori for a consistent and enterprise-ready user experience
  • Expose and consume APIs for seamless communication between frontend, AI services, and backend systems

The step journey, unpacked

The flow is built around four macro-phases — Initiate, Assess & Troubleshoot, Resolve, and Closure — each supported by a distinct set of SAP and partner tools.

Step 1: Self-service

The customer lands on a self-service portal before ever reaching a human. Intelligent Q&A, powered by eGain Knowledge Management, deflects simple queries instantly.

Step 2 : Case creation

When self-service can't resolve the issue, a case is created in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2. AI surfaces similar past cases and searches the knowledge base automatically.

Step 3: Opportunity

A service case becomes a commercial opportunity. Agents can pivot from problem-solving mode to solution-selling without switching systems.

Step 4 : Quote + DocuSign

A quote is generated and sent for e-signature via DocuSign — all within the same workflow. No email attachments, no context switching.

 

Step 5 : Solution order

In our Scenario we are using Sales order and Service Order

Once signed, the solution order in S/4HANA  is created and downstream fulfilment is triggered automatically.

 

Step 6 : Sales order fulfilment

SAP S/4HANA Cloud handles the sales order, delivery, and billing — a direct integration that eliminates re-keying of data.

Step 7 : Service order

In parallel, a service order is raised for any physical work that needs to happen on-site.

Step 8 : Service order replication

The service order is replicated into SAP Field Service Management, keeping every system consistent without manual effort.

Step 9 : Service call

A service call is initiated in SAP FSM, formally opening the field engagement.

 

Step 10 : Technician assignment

The right technician is assigned based on availability, skills, and location — the field team arrives prepared and resolve this issue

🏁Conclusion

This end-to-end flow demonstrates how organizations can transform customer journeys using AI and integrated enterprise platforms. By connecting sales, service, and backend operations, businesses can deliver faster, smarter, and more efficient customer experiences.
